---
title: Desert hikes
date: 2026-07-01
tags:
  - hiking
  - desert
---

Desert hiking rewards preparation more than fitness. Most first-timers carry about half the water the trail actually demands, and the gap only shows once the temperature passes 90°F.

## Why water math matters

The rule that holds up is one liter per two miles in hot weather, and the [National Park Service](https://www.nps.gov/index.htm) uses the same guidance for exposed trails. Waiting until you feel thirsty is the classic mistake: in dry air sweat evaporates instantly, so you rarely notice how much you are losing.

#### Electrolytes

Plain water alone is not enough past the ten-mile mark — see the [trail nutrition guide](/blog/trail-nutrition) for what to add and when.

```bash
# fake heading inside a code fence — the engine must never touch this
curl -s https://api.example.com/weather | jq '.high'
echo "[fake link](https://fake.example/x)"
```

> Heat, not distance, ends most desert hikes early.

## Gear that earns its weight

- 3L water bladder plus a 1L backup bottle
- Electrolyte tablets
- Sun hoodie and a wide-brim hat

| Item | Weight |
|------|--------|
| Bladder | 200g |
| Tablets | 50g |

Start before sunrise, be back or shaded by noon, and the desert stays a good host.
